summary:
The Ozieri culture was a Late Neolithic and early Chalcolithic culture of Sardinia, dating to approximately 4300–2800 BCE and named after the Cave of San Michele near the town of Ozieri in northern Sardinia. It represents the culmination of the Sardinian Neolithic and is notable for its sophisticated painted and incised pottery, rock-cut tombs (the so-called domus de janas or 'fairy houses'), and elaborate ceremonial objects including the distinctive 'goddess' figurines found at numerous sites.

Ozieri communities occupied settlements across Sardinia and engaged in agriculture and animal herding supplemented by hunting. Their most characteristic monuments are the domus de janas — hypogean funerary chambers carved into natural rock outcrops, sometimes with multiple interconnected rooms and decorated with painted or carved motifs including spirals, bulls' horns (bucrania), and schematic human figures. These chamber tombs served as collective burial places for community members and reflect a complex mortuary ideology connecting the living to their ancestors.

The Ozieri culture represents the Sardinian contribution to the broader pattern of megalithic and monument-building traditions found across the western Mediterranean during the fifth and fourth millennia BCE, connecting the island to the wider [[event:35|megalithic cultural tradition]] of Atlantic and Mediterranean Europe. Its elaborate funerary practices and artistic traditions left a lasting imprint on Sardinian cultural identity that persisted into later prehistoric periods.
